title: Quiz: HHL Algorithm for Linear Systems
description: Test your understanding of the Harrow-Hassidim-Lloyd algorithm structure, complexity, and applications

questions.question: What is the main output of the HHL algorithm?

options.text: The full classical solution vector x
A quantum state |x⟩ proportional to the solution
The eigenvalues of matrix A
The condition number of the system

questions.explanation: HHL outputs a quantum state |x⟩ that encodes the solution in its amplitudes, not the classical vector directly. This is both a feature (exponential compression) and a limitation (extraction requires measurement).

questions.question: What is the time complexity of HHL compared to classical Gaussian elimination?

options.text: O(N³) vs O(N³) - same complexity
O(N²) vs O(N³) - quadratic speedup
O(log N · poly(κ,s,1/ε)) vs O(N³) - exponential speedup in N
O(1) vs O(N³) - constant time

questions.explanation: HHL achieves O(log N · s²κ²/ε) complexity, exponentially faster in the dimension N. However, the dependence on condition number κ, sparsity s, and precision ε is polynomial.

questions.question: What is the role of quantum phase estimation in HHL?

options.text: Prepare the initial state |b⟩
Extract eigenvalues of A into the clock register
Perform the controlled rotation
Measure the final solution

questions.explanation: QPE extracts the eigenvalues λⱼ of A and encodes them as binary fractions in the clock register. This allows subsequent operations to access and manipulate these eigenvalues.

questions.question: How does HHL achieve eigenvalue inversion (computing 1/λ)?

options.text: By computing the inverse matrix A⁻¹ directly
Through controlled rotations that encode 1/λ in amplitude
Using quantum Fourier transform
By measuring eigenvalues and classical post-processing

questions.explanation: HHL uses controlled rotations R_y(arcsin(C/λ)) on an ancilla qubit. When post-selecting on ancilla |1⟩, the amplitude becomes proportional to C/λ, effectively inverting the eigenvalue.

questions.question: HHL requires the matrix A to be Hermitian (A = A†).

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True! HHL directly requires Hermitian matrices for the phase estimation step. Non-Hermitian matrices can be embedded into larger Hermitian systems, but the core algorithm assumes Hermiticity.

questions.question: What is the 'input problem' that limits HHL's practical advantage?

options.text: The matrix A must be sparse
Loading classical data into quantum state |b⟩ can cost O(N)
Only 2x2 matrices can be solved
The algorithm requires fault-tolerant quantum computers

questions.explanation: Preparing an arbitrary quantum state |b⟩ from classical data typically requires O(N) operations, which can negate the exponential speedup. HHL is most advantageous when |b⟩ is already quantum or has efficient structure.

questions.question: How does the condition number κ affect HHL's complexity?

options.text: No effect - HHL works equally well for all κ
Logarithmic dependence O(log κ)
Linear dependence O(κ)
Quadratic dependence O(κ²)

questions.explanation: HHL has O(κ²) complexity in the condition number, arising from the success probability of post-selection. Ill-conditioned matrices (large κ) significantly degrade performance.

questions.question: When does HHL provide a genuine quantum advantage? (Select all that apply)

options.text: When the result feeds into further quantum computation
When we only need to sample from the solution distribution
When we need every entry of the solution vector x
When the matrix is sparse and well-conditioned

questions.explanation: HHL is advantageous when: (a) output is used in quantum ML or other quantum algorithms, (b) sampling suffices rather than full extraction, (d) matrix properties (sparse, well-conditioned) are favorable. Reading all N entries (c) requires O(N) measurements, losing the advantage.
